annotations.label: Point (geometry)

anchor.snippet: a point is an abstract idealization of an exact position

annotations.note: An informal abstract idealization, not a single Mathlib declaration; Mathlib uses elements of various spaces (sets, affine spaces, etc.) but has no overarching `Point` definition.

annotations.label: Point as primitive notion

anchor.snippet: a point is a primitive notion , defined as "that which has no part"

annotations.note: Philosophical/Euclidean primitive notion with no direct Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Vertex / corner

anchor.snippet: A point can also be determined by the intersection of two curves or three surfaces, called a vertex or corner

annotations.note: No general intersection-vertex/corner definition exists in Mathlib at this informal level.

annotations.label: Point set

anchor.snippet: In modern mathematics, a space of points is typically treated as a set , a point set

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Set` type captures a point set as a set of elements.

annotations.label: Isolated point

anchor.snippet: An isolated point is an element of some subset of points which has some neighborhood containing no other points of the subset

annotations.note: Mathlib expresses isolation via `Filter.NeBot (𝓝[≠] x)` (perfect-space context) rather than a standalone `IsIsolatedPoint` predicate.

annotations.label: Point in the Euclidean plane

anchor.snippet: a point is represented by an ordered pair ( x , y ) of numbers

annotations.note: Points in the Euclidean plane are elements of `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)`.

annotations.label: Point in n-dimensional space

anchor.snippet: Further generalizations are represented by an ordered tuplet of n terms

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace 𝕜 (Fin n)` realizes points as ordered n-tuples.

annotations.label: Line as a set of points

anchor.snippet: a line is an infinite set of points of the form

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes lines as one-dimensional affine subspaces (a special case of `AffineSubspace`).

annotations.label: Degenerate line segment

anchor.snippet: A line segment consisting of only a single point is called a degenerate line segment

annotations.note: No `DegenerateSegment` predicate; degenerate segments are not given a dedicated name in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two points determine a line

anchor.snippet: any two points can be connected by a straight line

annotations.note: Mathlib has `affineSpan` of two points but no single canonical statement equivalent to Euclid's `two points determine a line` axiom.

annotations.label: Point is 0-dimensional

anchor.snippet: In all of the common definitions, a point is 0-dimensional

annotations.note: Mathlib proves dimension zero for a point under the Hausdorff-dimension and rank notions, but not a single unified `point is 0-dimensional` statement covering all dimension theories.

annotations.label: Vector space dimension

anchor.snippet: The dimension of a vector space is the maximum size of a linearly independent subset

annotations.note: `Module.rank` (and its finite cousin `Module.finrank`) is the dimension of a module/vector space.

annotations.label: Single-point vector space has no linearly independent subset

anchor.snippet: In a vector space consisting of a single point

annotations.note: `rank_subsingleton'` proves `Module.rank R M = 0` whenever `M` is subsingleton, e.g. the trivial vector space.

annotations.label: Topological (covering) dimension

anchor.snippet: The topological dimension of a topological space

annotations.note: Lebesgue covering dimension is not defined in Mathlib (no `topologicalDimension`/`coveringDimension`).

annotations.label: Infinite covering dimension

anchor.snippet: If no such minimal n exists, the space is said to be of infinite covering dimension

annotations.note: Depends on covering dimension, which is itself not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Point has covering dimension zero

anchor.snippet: A point is zero-dimensional with respect to the covering dimension

annotations.note: No covering dimension is defined in Mathlib, so this statement is not stated there.

annotations.label: Hausdorff content

anchor.snippet: the d -dimensional Hausdorff content of S is the infimum of the set of numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Hausdorff measure `μH[d]` (built via `mkMetric`); the unrestricted-diameter Hausdorff content is not separately named.

annotations.label: Hausdorff dimension

anchor.snippet: The Hausdorff dimension of X is defined by

annotations.note: `dimH` is the Hausdorff dimension of a set in a (pseudo-)e-metric space.

annotations.label: Point has Hausdorff dimension 0

anchor.snippet: A point has Hausdorff dimension 0 because it can be covered by a single ball of arbitrarily small radius

annotations.note: `dimH_singleton` states `dimH {x} = 0`.

annotations.label: Pointless space

anchor.snippet: A "pointless" or "pointfree" space is defined not as a set

annotations.note: Mathlib has frames/locales-adjacent material (e.g. `Order.Frame`) but no `Pointless`/`Pointfree` space construct as a standalone concept.

annotations.label: Dirac delta function

anchor.section: Point masses and the Dirac delta function
anchor.snippet: is (informally) a generalized function on the real number line that is zero everywhere except at zero, with an integral of one over the entire real line

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the Dirac delta as the Dirac measure `Measure.dirac a` (and as a tempered distribution in `Analysis.Distribution.TemperedDistribution`).

annotations.label: Kronecker delta function

anchor.section: Point masses and the Dirac delta function
anchor.snippet: Its discrete analog is the Kronecker delta function which is usually defined on a finite domain and takes values 0 and 1

annotations.note: Mathlib has Kronecker matrix products but no dedicated `kroneckerDelta` function; uses `if i = j then 1 else 0` (e.g. via `Matrix.one_apply`) ad hoc.
